title: Tricyclic Antidepressant Poisoning
oneLiner: A potentially lethal overdose causing anticholinergic toxidrome, widened QRS, seizures, and ventricular arrhythmias – requires aggressive cardiovascular and neurological support.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation (Torsade de Pointes / polymorphic VT):** Look for **QRS > 140 ms** especially in leads aVR (R wave > 3 mm) or aVR terminal R wave — this is the immediate trigger for sodium bicarbonate.
- **Generalised tonic-clonic seizure with airway compromise:** Onset can be abrupt; **single prodrome of agitation or myoclonus** — do not wait for formal EEG. Secure airway early if GCS < 9 or ongoing seizures.
- **Hypotension with refractory AMS:** TCA-induced myocardial depression + vasodilation; **hypotension unresponsive to fluids + sodium bicarbonate** — needs early vasopressor (noradrenaline) and ICU referral.
sections.Presentation:
- **History:** Deliberate self-harm (commonly amitriptyline, dosulepin, imipramine); accidental in elderly (anticholinergic load); time of ingestion (peak effect 1-4h, but risk up to 12h with delayed gut absorption/sustained-release).
- **Cardinal symptoms (3-5):**
  - Anticholinergic: dry mouth, blurred vision, flushed skin, urinary retention, hyperthermia
  - CNS depression: confusion, drowsiness, coma
  - Seizures (generalised, may recur)
  - Cardiac: palpitations, syncope, chest tightness
  - Respiratory: bradypnoea, aspiration risk
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Warm, dry, flushed skin** (anticholinergic)
  - Dilated pupils (mydriasis)
  - Tachycardia (early) or bradycardia (late/ominous)
  - Hypotension
  - Decreased GCS + agitation or myoclonus
  - ECG: sinus tachycardia, prolonged QRS (>100 ms concerning, >140 ms high risk), prolonged QT, right axis deviation, R wave in aVR > 3 mm, Brugada-like pattern
  - Seizures: usually generalised tonic-clonic, may be status epilepticus
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Cocaine / amphetamine overdose:** sympathomimetic toxidrome (tachycardia, hypertension, seizures, but *sweating, mydriasis with constricted? No, cocaine: dilated pupils, but skin is cool/clammy, not dry*). **Splitter:** **Warm dry skin points to TCA; cool clammy prefers sympathomimetic.**
- **Serotonin syndrome:** hyperthermia, clonus, hyperreflexia (vs TCA: anticholinergic with dry skin, absent clonus, decreased bowel sounds).
- **Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS):** lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, autonomic instability (vs TCA: no rigidity, anticholinergic features).
- **Other sedative overdose (benzodiazepines, opioids):** miosis, low respiratory rate, normal bowel sounds, normal skin (not anticholinergic). **Splitter: Pupils miotic vs mydriatic + skin dry vs moist.**
- **Organophosphate poisoning:** excessive salivation, lacrimation, urination, diaphoresis, miosis – opposite of anticholinergic.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **ECG stat** (repeat every 15 min until stable then hourly for 6h) – measure QRS width, QTc, aVR R wave height
  - Capillary blood glucose (hypoglycaemia can mimic)
  - Pulse oximetry + capnography (if intubated)
  - 12-lead continuous monitoring
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs (lactic acidosis from seizures/hypotension)
  - Paracetamol + salicylate level (co-ingestion)
  - TCA serum levels **not routinely helpful** in acute management; only confirmatory if unclear history (therapeutic range 50-150 ng/mL; toxic >500 ng/mL)
  - ABG: metabolic acidosis (lactic) – may need bicarbonate
- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray only if aspiration suspected or for line positioning (not routine)
- **Special tests:** None
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **TCA toxicity severity:**
    - Mild: anticholinergic features only, QRS <100 ms
    - Moderate: QRS 100–140 ms, drowsy but rousable
    - Severe: QRS >140 ms, seizures, hypotension, coma
  - **aVR sign (terminal R wave ≥ 3 mm):** sensitive for severe cardiotoxicity (positive predictive value ~80%)
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – omission in any overdose with altered mental status or tachycardia is a serious error. Also **serum paracetamol level** in all self-harm presentations.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Airway management:** If GCS < 9, seizures ongoing, or aspiration risk – intubate early (rapid sequence induction; avoid etomidate if hypotensive, use ketamine cautiously – may lower seizure threshold; consider propofol by anaesthetist).
  - **IV access** x 2 wide bore, monitor
  - **Activated charcoal** 50 g PO/NG only if ≤ 1 hour post-ingestion, airway protected, and no contraindications (ileus, reduced GCS).
  - **Sodium bicarbonate (8.4%):** Indicated for QRS > 140 ms OR ventricular arrhythmias OR haemodynamic instability. Give **1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus (approx 50-100 mL of 8.4% sodium bicarbonate over 10-20 minutes)**, repeat if QRS not narrowing after 5-10 min. Target QRS < 120 ms. Can follow with continuous infusion (50-100 mmol in 1L 5% dextrose or 0.45% saline at 100-200 mL/h) [check local policy for specific infusion protocol].
  - **Seizures:** Treat with IV **lorazepam** 2-4 mg IV or **diazepam** 5-10 mg IV (may repeat). If refractory, **phenobarbital** 15 mg/kg IV at 50 mg/min [check local policy]. Avoid phenytoin (ineffective, may worsen arrhythmias).
  - **Hypotension:** Initially **500-1000 mL crystalloid** (caution if pulmonary oedema from myocardial depression). If persistent: start **noradrenaline** infusion (MCQ: dobutamine may worsen hypotension due to vasodilation). Consider **sodium bicarbonate** for acidotic/arrhythmic hypotension.
  - **Cardiac arrhythmias:** For polymorphic VT (Torsade): defibrillation, then IV magnesium 1-2 g over 5-10 minutes, and continue sodium bicarbonate. Avoid class IA/IC antiarrhythmics (e.g., lidocaine may aggravate). Amiodarone can be used for stable VT after bicarbonate [check local policy].
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit to **ICU/HDU** if: QRS > 120 ms after initial bicarbonate, seizures, hypotension, GCS < 10, any arrhythmia, or requiring intubation.
  - Continuous ECG monitoring for minimum 12-24 hours after last sodium bicarbonate or until QRS < 100 ms for 6 hours.
  - Fluid balance chart; monitor for anticholinergic ileus (NG tube if vomiting/obtunded).
  - Repeat electrolytes, ABG if acidosis persists.
  - Consider **lipid emulsion (Intralipid 20%)** for refractory cardiotoxicity – 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion (used in lipid-soluble drug poisonings) – discuss with regional poisons unit.
  - Psychiatry/mental health liaison early (SAAD assessment).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** After medical clearance, all patients should be assessed by psychiatric team before discharge. Provide crisis plan and follow-up.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% (1 mmol/mL):** 1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus (repeat as per QRS). For infusion: 100 mmol (100 mL of 8.4%) in 1L 5% dextrose at 100-200 mL/h [check local policy].
- **Activated charcoal:** 50 g single dose PO/NG (if < 1h, airway protected).
- **Lorazepam:** 2-4 mg IV slowly, repeat PRN (for seizures/agitation).
- **Diazepam:** 5-10 mg IV (slow) for seizures; watch for respiratory depression.
- **Noradrenaline:** Start 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP > 65 mmHg.
- **Magnesium sulfate:** 1-2 g IV over 5-10 min for Torsade de Pointes.
- **Lipid emulsion (Intralipid 20%):** 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus, then 0.25 mL/kg/min for 30-60 min (used as rescue for lipophilic drug toxicity) – contact poisons unit.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Sodium bicarbonate: avoid in metabolic alkalosis (rare), monitor K+ (bicarbonate shifts K+ intracellularly – hypokalaemia can worsen arrhythmias; replete K+ if low). Activated charcoal: do not give if ileus, bowel obstruction, or unprotected airway.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific changes for these drugs; monitor renal function for metabolic acidosis.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving sodium bicarbonate too fast or too slow** – rapid push can cause hypokalaemic arrest; too slow may not reverse QRS widening in time. **Also**: using **lidocaine** for TCA-induced arrhythmia – proarrhythmic; and **phenytoin** for seizures – ineffective, proarrhythmic.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Ventricular fibrillation, asystole
  - Refractory seizures → status epilepticus, rhabdomyolysis, anoxic brain injury
  - Aspiration pneumonia (reduced GCS or seizures)
  - Metabolic acidosis (lactic)
  - Acute respiratory distress syndrome (from prolonged shock/aspiration)
  - Hyperthermia (anticholinergic)
- **Chronic:**
  - Anoxic brain injury (prolonged hypotension or hypoglycaemia)
  - Persistent anticholinergic symptoms (confusion, constipation, urinary retention) for days in elderly
  - Post-seizure myopathy, acute kidney injury (rhabdomyolysis)
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality ~1-3% in hospitalised overdoses; higher with co-ingestion or delayed presentation. Most deaths occur within first 6 hours from arrhythmia. Full recovery expected if no anoxic injury. Patients with QRS > 140 & hypotension have higher mortality.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Get a good early ECG** – repeat every 15 minutes until QRS stable. The aVR R wave height is often the most sensitive sign.
- **Give sodium bicarbonate early** – do not wait for arrhythmia; start when QRS > 140 ms or if R wave in aVR > 3 mm.
- **Contact your local poisons unit early** (e.g., National Poisons Information Service (NPIS) in UK – check local number) for advice on refractory cases or lipid emulsion.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give flumazenil** – can precipitate seizures in TCA overdose (different mechanism).
- **Do not wait for TCA serum levels** – they do not guide acute management; clinical features and ECG are what matter.
- **Do not discharge after apparent resolution** – recurrence of QRS widening can occur up to 12h post-ingestion due to delayed gut absorption. At least 6h of monitoring with normal ECG is required before medical clearance; 12h if sustained-release preparation.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening drowsiness, chest pain/palpitations, new weakness, or seizure.
- **Follow-up:** Psychiatric team within 48 hours. GP review in 1 week for medication reconciliation (if restarting antidepressants, choose a safer agent like SSRI). Provide crisis contact number (Samaritans, crisis team). Consider referral to local drug & alcohol service if substance misuse.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (SpR/Consultant) if:** QRS > 140 ms despite initial bicarbonate; refractory seizures; any ventricular arrhythmia; GCS < 9; need for intubation.
- **Call ICU if:** Requirement for noradrenaline > 0.3 mcg/kg/min; refractory acidosis; status epilepticus; any vasopressor-dependent hypotension.
- **Crash team (2222) if:** Pulseless, seizure with airway compromised, or cardiac arrest.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NEWS2:** ≥ 5 triggers escalation to MEWS/review; ≥ 7 triggers urgent medical review.
  - **Lactate:** > 4 mmol/L with hypotension – red flag for shock.
  - **GCS:** drop to < 9 – consider airway protection.

rawMarkdown:
## Tricyclic Antidepressant Poisoning
**One-liner:** A potentially lethal overdose causing anticholinergic toxidrome, widened QRS, seizures, and ventricular arrhythmias – requires aggressive cardiovascular and neurological support.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation (Torsade de Pointes / polymorphic VT):** Look for **QRS > 140 ms** especially in leads aVR (R wave > 3 mm) or aVR terminal R wave — this is the immediate trigger for sodium bicarbonate.
- **Generalised tonic-clonic seizure with airway compromise:** Onset can be abrupt; **single prodrome of agitation or myoclonus** — do not wait for formal EEG. Secure airway early if GCS < 9 or ongoing seizures.
- **Hypotension with refractory AMS:** TCA-induced myocardial depression + vasodilation; **hypotension unresponsive to fluids + sodium bicarbonate** — needs early vasopressor (noradrenaline) and ICU referral.

### Presentation
- **History:** Deliberate self-harm (commonly amitriptyline, dosulepin, imipramine); accidental in elderly (anticholinergic load); time of ingestion (peak effect 1-4h, but risk up to 12h with delayed gut absorption/sustained-release).
- **Cardinal symptoms (3-5):**
  - Anticholinergic: dry mouth, blurred vision, flushed skin, urinary retention, hyperthermia
  - CNS depression: confusion, drowsiness, coma
  - Seizures (generalised, may recur)
  - Cardiac: palpitations, syncope, chest tightness
  - Respiratory: bradypnoea, aspiration risk
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Warm, dry, flushed skin** (anticholinergic)
  - Dilated pupils (mydriasis)
  - Tachycardia (early) or bradycardia (late/ominous)
  - Hypotension
  - Decreased GCS + agitation or myoclonus
  - ECG: sinus tachycardia, prolonged QRS (>100 ms concerning, >140 ms high risk), prolonged QT, right axis deviation, R wave in aVR > 3 mm, Brugada-like pattern
  - Seizures: usually generalised tonic-clonic, may be status epilepticus

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Cocaine / amphetamine overdose:** sympathomimetic toxidrome (tachycardia, hypertension, seizures, but *sweating, mydriasis with constricted? No, cocaine: dilated pupils, but skin is cool/clammy, not dry*). **Splitter:** **Warm dry skin points to TCA; cool clammy prefers sympathomimetic.**
- **Serotonin syndrome:** hyperthermia, clonus, hyperreflexia (vs TCA: anticholinergic with dry skin, absent clonus, decreased bowel sounds).
- **Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS):** lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, autonomic instability (vs TCA: no rigidity, anticholinergic features).
- **Other sedative overdose (benzodiazepines, opioids):** miosis, low respiratory rate, normal bowel sounds, normal skin (not anticholinergic). **Splitter: Pupils miotic vs mydriatic + skin dry vs moist.**
- **Organophosphate poisoning:** excessive salivation, lacrimation, urination, diaphoresis, miosis – opposite of anticholinergic.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **ECG stat** (repeat every 15 min until stable then hourly for 6h) – measure QRS width, QTc, aVR R wave height
  - Capillary blood glucose (hypoglycaemia can mimic)
  - Pulse oximetry + capnography (if intubated)
  - 12-lead continuous monitoring
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs (lactic acidosis from seizures/hypotension)
  - Paracetamol + salicylate level (co-ingestion)
  - TCA serum levels **not routinely helpful** in acute management; only confirmatory if unclear history (therapeutic range 50-150 ng/mL; toxic >500 ng/mL)
  - ABG: metabolic acidosis (lactic) – may need bicarbonate
- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray only if aspiration suspected or for line positioning (not routine)
- **Special tests:** None
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **TCA toxicity severity:**
    - Mild: anticholinergic features only, QRS <100 ms
    - Moderate: QRS 100–140 ms, drowsy but rousable
    - Severe: QRS >140 ms, seizures, hypotension, coma
  - **aVR sign (terminal R wave ≥ 3 mm):** sensitive for severe cardiotoxicity (positive predictive value ~80%)
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – omission in any overdose with altered mental status or tachycardia is a serious error. Also **serum paracetamol level** in all self-harm presentations.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Airway management:** If GCS < 9, seizures ongoing, or aspiration risk – intubate early (rapid sequence induction; avoid etomidate if hypotensive, use ketamine cautiously – may lower seizure threshold; consider propofol by anaesthetist).
  - **IV access** x 2 wide bore, monitor
  - **Activated charcoal** 50 g PO/NG only if ≤ 1 hour post-ingestion, airway protected, and no contraindications (ileus, reduced GCS).
  - **Sodium bicarbonate (8.4%):** Indicated for QRS > 140 ms OR ventricular arrhythmias OR haemodynamic instability. Give **1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus (approx 50-100 mL of 8.4% sodium bicarbonate over 10-20 minutes)**, repeat if QRS not narrowing after 5-10 min. Target QRS < 120 ms. Can follow with continuous infusion (50-100 mmol in 1L 5% dextrose or 0.45% saline at 100-200 mL/h) [check local policy for specific infusion protocol].
  - **Seizures:** Treat with IV **lorazepam** 2-4 mg IV or **diazepam** 5-10 mg IV (may repeat). If refractory, **phenobarbital** 15 mg/kg IV at 50 mg/min [check local policy]. Avoid phenytoin (ineffective, may worsen arrhythmias).
  - **Hypotension:** Initially **500-1000 mL crystalloid** (caution if pulmonary oedema from myocardial depression). If persistent: start **noradrenaline** infusion (MCQ: dobutamine may worsen hypotension due to vasodilation). Consider **sodium bicarbonate** for acidotic/arrhythmic hypotension.
  - **Cardiac arrhythmias:** For polymorphic VT (Torsade): defibrillation, then IV magnesium 1-2 g over 5-10 minutes, and continue sodium bicarbonate. Avoid class IA/IC antiarrhythmics (e.g., lidocaine may aggravate). Amiodarone can be used for stable VT after bicarbonate [check local policy].
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit to **ICU/HDU** if: QRS > 120 ms after initial bicarbonate, seizures, hypotension, GCS < 10, any arrhythmia, or requiring intubation.
  - Continuous ECG monitoring for minimum 12-24 hours after last sodium bicarbonate or until QRS < 100 ms for 6 hours.
  - Fluid balance chart; monitor for anticholinergic ileus (NG tube if vomiting/obtunded).
  - Repeat electrolytes, ABG if acidosis persists.
  - Consider **lipid emulsion (Intralipid 20%)** for refractory cardiotoxicity – 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion (used in lipid-soluble drug poisonings) – discuss with regional poisons unit.
  - Psychiatry/mental health liaison early (SAAD assessment).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** After medical clearance, all patients should be assessed by psychiatric team before discharge. Provide crisis plan and follow-up.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% (1 mmol/mL):** 1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus (repeat as per QRS). For infusion: 100 mmol (100 mL of 8.4%) in 1L 5% dextrose at 100-200 mL/h [check local policy].
- **Activated charcoal:** 50 g single dose PO/NG (if < 1h, airway protected).
- **Lorazepam:** 2-4 mg IV slowly, repeat PRN (for seizures/agitation).
- **Diazepam:** 5-10 mg IV (slow) for seizures; watch for respiratory depression.
- **Noradrenaline:** Start 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP > 65 mmHg.
- **Magnesium sulfate:** 1-2 g IV over 5-10 min for Torsade de Pointes.
- **Lipid emulsion (Intralipid 20%):** 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus, then 0.25 mL/kg/min for 30-60 min (used as rescue for lipophilic drug toxicity) – contact poisons unit.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Sodium bicarbonate: avoid in metabolic alkalosis (rare), monitor K+ (bicarbonate shifts K+ intracellularly – hypokalaemia can worsen arrhythmias; replete K+ if low). Activated charcoal: do not give if ileus, bowel obstruction, or unprotected airway.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific changes for these drugs; monitor renal function for metabolic acidosis.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving sodium bicarbonate too fast or too slow** – rapid push can cause hypokalaemic arrest; too slow may not reverse QRS widening in time. **Also**: using **lidocaine** for TCA-induced arrhythmia – proarrhythmic; and **phenytoin** for seizures – ineffective, proarrhythmic.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Ventricular fibrillation, asystole
  - Refractory seizures → status epilepticus, rhabdomyolysis, anoxic brain injury
  - Aspiration pneumonia (reduced GCS or seizures)
  - Metabolic acidosis (lactic)
  - Acute respiratory distress syndrome (from prolonged shock/aspiration)
  - Hyperthermia (anticholinergic)
- **Chronic:**
  - Anoxic brain injury (prolonged hypotension or hypoglycaemia)
  - Persistent anticholinergic symptoms (confusion, constipation, urinary retention) for days in elderly
  - Post-seizure myopathy, acute kidney injury (rhabdomyolysis)

### Prognosis
- Mortality ~1-3% in hospitalised overdoses; higher with co-ingestion or delayed presentation. Most deaths occur within first 6 hours from arrhythmia. Full recovery expected if no anoxic injury. Patients with QRS > 140 & hypotension have higher mortality.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Get a good early ECG** – repeat every 15 minutes until QRS stable. The aVR R wave height is often the most sensitive sign.
- **Give sodium bicarbonate early** – do not wait for arrhythmia; start when QRS > 140 ms or if R wave in aVR > 3 mm.
- **Contact your local poisons unit early** (e.g., National Poisons Information Service (NPIS) in UK – check local number) for advice on refractory cases or lipid emulsion.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give flumazenil** – can precipitate seizures in TCA overdose (different mechanism).
- **Do not wait for TCA serum levels** – they do not guide acute management; clinical features and ECG are what matter.
- **Do not discharge after apparent resolution** – recurrence of QRS widening can occur up to 12h post-ingestion due to delayed gut absorption. At least 6h of monitoring with normal ECG is required before medical clearance; 12h if sustained-release preparation.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening drowsiness, chest pain/palpitations, new weakness, or seizure.
- **Follow-up:** Psychiatric team within 48 hours. GP review in 1 week for medication reconciliation (if restarting antidepressants, choose a safer agent like SSRI). Provide crisis contact number (Samaritans, crisis team). Consider referral to local drug & alcohol service if substance misuse.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (SpR/Consultant) if:** QRS > 140 ms despite initial bicarbonate; refractory seizures; any ventricular arrhythmia; GCS < 9; need for intubation.
- **Call ICU if:** Requirement for noradrenaline > 0.3 mcg/kg/min; refractory acidosis; status epilepticus; any vasopressor-dependent hypotension.
- **Crash team (2222) if:** Pulseless, seizure with airway compromised, or cardiac arrest.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NEWS2:** ≥ 5 triggers escalation to MEWS/review; ≥ 7 triggers urgent medical review.
  - **Lactate:** > 4 mmol/L with hypotension – red flag for shock.
  - **GCS:** drop to < 9 – consider airway protection.
